quizQuestion: What property of matter equals mass divided by volume, with SI unit kg/m³?
explanation: Density ρ = m/V. SI unit: kg/m³. Water has ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³; air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³; iron ≈ 7900 kg/m³. Objects denser than the surrounding fluid sink; less dense objects float. Density is an intrinsic property — a small piece and a large piece of the same material have identical density.
visualDescription: A cube of material with mass m and volume V labeled, showing ρ = m/V

quizQuestion: What quantity equals the perpendicular force exerted per unit area on a surface, measured in pascals (Pa)?
explanation: Pressure P = F/A. Only the component of force perpendicular to the surface counts. SI unit: 1 Pa = 1 N/m². Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa. Pressure acts equally in all directions in a static fluid. A sharp needle concentrates force on a tiny area → very high pressure → penetrates skin.
visualDescription: A force F applied over area A, with P = F/A labeled, and a comparison of wide vs. narrow tip showing pressure difference

acceptableAlternatives: pressure increases with depth as rho g h
quizQuestion: What equation gives the pressure at depth h in a static fluid with surface pressure P₀?
explanation: P = P₀ + ρgh. Pressure increases linearly with depth. At depth h: the column of fluid above exerts pressure ρgh in addition to the surface pressure P₀. In the ocean, every 10 m of depth adds approximately 1 atm (≈ 100 kPa) of pressure (seawater ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³, g ≈ 10 m/s²: ρgh ≈ 1025 × 10 × 10 ≈ 100,000 Pa).
visualDescription: A tank of fluid with depth h labeled, showing pressure P₀ at the surface and P = P₀ + ρgh at the bottom

acceptableAlternatives: pressure in enclosed fluid transmits equally in all directions
quizQuestion: What principle states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to every point in the fluid?
explanation: Pascal's Principle: a pressure change ΔP applied at any point in a confined fluid transmits fully (undiminished) to all other parts. This is the basis of hydraulic systems: applying small force over small area creates pressure that multiplies force on a large-area piston (F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂). Used in car brake systems, hydraulic jacks, and dentist chairs.
visualDescription: A hydraulic jack with small piston (small area, small force) connected to large piston (large area, large force)

acceptableAlternatives: buoyant force equals rho times V times g
quizQuestion: What equation gives the buoyant force on an object submerged in or floating on a fluid?
explanation: F_b = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g (Archimedes' Principle). Buoyancy equals the weight of the fluid displaced. An object floats when F_b ≥ F_g, which means ρ_object ≤ ρ_fluid. A submerged object: buoyant force = ρ_fluid × V_object × g. For a floating object: ρ_fluid × V_displaced = ρ_object × V_object (the submerged fraction = ρ_object/ρ_fluid).
visualDescription: An iceberg with a small portion above water and a large portion below, labeled with buoyancy and weight forces

acceptableAlternatives: the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid
quizQuestion: What principle states that the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces?
explanation: Archimedes' Principle: F_b = weight of displaced fluid = ρ_fluid V_displaced g. This explains flotation, submarine depth control, and the apparent weight loss of objects in water. According to legend, Archimedes discovered this while stepping into a bathtub and shouted 'Eureka!' Ships float because their large hollow hull displaces enough water to support their weight.
visualDescription: Archimedes overflowing a bathtub with text 'Eureka!' and the equation F_b = ρ_fluid V g

acceptableAlternatives: flow rate is constant: A times v is constant
quizQuestion: What equation expresses conservation of mass for an incompressible fluid flowing through a pipe?
explanation: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂: the volume flow rate Q = Av is constant. If a pipe narrows (A₂ < A₁), the fluid speeds up (v₂ > v₁) to maintain the same flow rate. This explains why water from a garden hose speeds up when you partly cover the nozzle. The continuity equation assumes incompressible, steady flow.
visualDescription: A pipe that narrows from area A₁ to A₂, with fluid moving faster in the narrower section (v₂ > v₁)

acceptableAlternatives: pressure plus dynamic pressure plus hydrostatic pressure is constant
quizQuestion: What equation expresses conservation of energy for an ideal fluid flowing along a streamline?
explanation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant (Bernoulli's equation). This is conservation of energy per unit volume: pressure energy + kinetic energy density + gravitational PE density. Faster fluid has lower pressure (½ρv² increases, P must decrease). This explains airplane lift (faster air over wing → lower pressure on top → net upward force) and the Venturi effect.
visualDescription: A cross-section of an airplane wing with faster airflow on top (lower P) and slower below (higher P) creating lift

quizQuestion: What principle states that for a flowing fluid, regions of higher velocity have lower pressure?
explanation: Bernoulli's Principle: in a steady flow, faster-moving fluid has lower pressure (from P + ½ρv² = constant when h is constant). Applications: airplane lift, Venturi meter (measuring flow speed), atomizers/sprayers, and the curveball in baseball (Magnus effect). Named after Daniel Bernoulli (1700–1782).
visualDescription: A garden hose nozzle with high-speed flow and low-pressure region labeled

quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of pressure, and how is it expressed in base units?
explanation: 1 Pa (pascal) = 1 N/m² = 1 kg/(m⋅s²). Named after Blaise Pascal. Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa ≈ 101.3 kPa. A gentle breeze creates about 1-2 Pa. A car tire is inflated to about 200,000 Pa (200 kPa) gauge pressure. Blood pressure is measured in mmHg (1 mmHg ≈ 133 Pa).
visualDescription: Pressure gauge showing units of Pa = N/m² with atmospheric pressure labeled as ~101 kPa

correctAnswer: Object floats when its average density ≤ fluid density
quizQuestion: What determines whether an object floats or sinks in a fluid?
explanation: An object floats if its average density ρ_object ≤ ρ_fluid; it sinks if ρ_object > ρ_fluid. Average density matters — a steel ship floats because the hull encloses air, making the ship's average density less than water. Ice floats on water because ρ_ice (917 kg/m³) < ρ_water (1000 kg/m³). Quantitatively: submerged fraction = ρ_object/ρ_fluid.
visualDescription: Three objects: one sinking (dense), one floating partially submerged (same density as fraction submerged), one on surface (less dense)
distractors: Object floats when buoyant force equals gravity always
Object floats when volume is large enough
Object sinks only if denser than steel
Object floats if surface area is large
Object floats when mass is less than 1 kg
Object floats when normal force exceeds weight
Dense objects never float under any conditions

correctAnswer: Gauge pressure = absolute pressure − atmospheric pressure
quizQuestion: What is gauge pressure, and how does it differ from absolute pressure?
explanation: Gauge pressure = P_absolute − P_atm. Most everyday pressure measurements (tire pressure, blood pressure) are gauge pressures — they measure pressure ABOVE atmospheric. A tire gauge reads 0 when deflated (at atmospheric pressure). For physics problems, use absolute pressure when calculating hydrostatic pressure or applying Bernoulli's equation.
visualDescription: A tire pressure gauge showing 32 psi gauge, with atmospheric pressure (14.7 psi) labeled as the baseline
distractors: Gauge pressure is always larger than absolute pressure
Absolute pressure is only used underwater
Gauge pressure equals weight per area

correctAnswer: The fluid pushes back with an equal and opposite force (Newton's Third Law)
acceptableAlternatives: object pushes fluid down; fluid pushes object up
quizQuestion: How does Newton's Third Law explain why a submerged object experiences a buoyant force?
explanation: The object pushes down on the surrounding fluid (by its weight), and by Newton's Third Law, the fluid pushes back on the object upward. This upward reaction force is the buoyant force. Equivalently, the fluid pressure acts on all surfaces of the object — net upward pressure force because P increases with depth (bottom face has higher pressure than top face).
visualDescription: A submerged block with pressure arrows on all surfaces, net upward force labeled as buoyancy from Newton's Third Law
distractors: Buoyancy comes from the object's weight pressing on the bottom
Buoyancy is due to the object's temperature
Newton's Third Law doesn't apply to fluids
The fluid is compressed by the object creating upward force
Buoyancy exists only in liquids, not gases
Buoyancy is equal to the object's weight always
Buoyancy results from hydrostatic equilibrium of the object

acceptableAlternatives: flow rate = cross-sectional area times fluid speed
quizQuestion: What equation gives the volume flow rate Q (volume per unit time) through a pipe in terms of cross-sectional area A and fluid speed v?
explanation: Q = Av, where Q has units of m³/s. The continuity equation says Q = A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ = constant for incompressible steady flow. A large pipe (large A) with slow flow can carry the same volume per second as a narrow pipe (small A) with fast flow. Flow rate is conserved (like current in a circuit — charge conservation analog).
visualDescription: A pipe of area A with fluid moving at speed v, labeled Q = Av = constant throughout the pipe
